body:
🚀Spring Boot: A full REST API on an H2 in-memory database — no Docker, nothing to install.

✅ H2 lives inside the app's memory: the database is created on startup and thrown away on shutdown — ideal for demos and tests
✅ Extend JpaRepository and you get full CRUD (list, fetch, create, update, delete) without writing SQL
✅ Just run the app — the schema and sample rows load automatically, and you can browse them at /h2-console

🚀Spring Cloud: Centralize config with Spring Cloud Config before you copy application.properties everywhere.

✅ Centralize config with Spring Cloud Config before you copy application.properties everywhere
✅ Runnable sample: `spring-cloud-config-native-server`
✅ Architecture diagram + Carbon CodePen below

🚀Spring Cloud: Eureka service discovery — call services by name, not IP.

✅ Each service registers with Eureka on startup (spring.application.name)
✅ Eureka tracks healthy instances via heartbeats
✅ Clients call http://author-service/... — LoadBalancer resolves the name to a live instance

🚀Spring Cloud: OpenFeign + Circuit Breaker — resilience isn't an afterthought.

✅ @FeignClient calls remote services by name (via Eureka + LoadBalancer)
✅ @CircuitBreaker stops calling a failing service after a threshold
✅ fallbackMethod returns a safe default while the circuit is OPEN

🚀Spring Cloud: Contract testing with Pact — make sure two services still agree on their API.

✅ The consumer writes down the response it expects and tests against a mock — the real provider doesn't need to be running
✅ The provider replays that same contract against the real app and fails if it no longer matches
✅ Each side is verified separately, so a breaking change shows up long before deployment

🚀Spring Cloud: Load testing with Gatling — check a service survives real traffic.

✅ Describe a scenario (e.g. POST /library/book) and ramp virtual users up over time
✅ Feed randomized data with Faker so every request looks realistic
✅ Assert SLAs on the whole run — fail it if it gets too slow (≤10s) or drops below >90% success

💡 Java 17 — sealed types close the inheritance graph with `permits`.

✅ Only listed subtypes may extend the sealed class/interface
✅ Each permitted subtype must be `final`, `sealed`, or `non-sealed`
✅ `non-sealed` is the escape hatch — inheritance opens again below that branch

💡 Java 14+ switch expressions: the compiler checks exhaustiveness for you.

✅ Skip an enum constant → compile error
✅ Fix it by handling every value or adding `default`
✅ Fewer forgotten branches when enums evolve

💡 VarHandle fences in plain English (a lighter alternative to `volatile`) 🧵

✅ When two threads share a variable, the CPU can reorder and cache writes. One thread updates a value, the other keeps seeing the old one. That's the bug VarHandle fences help you avoid. 🧵

✅ `volatile` fixes it by fully syncing every read and write. Safe, but it's the heavy hammer — often stronger (and slower) than you actually need. 🧵

✅ setRelease = "publish". The writer says: "I'm done. Everything I wrote before this is now safe to read." Like hitting Save + Publish on a post. 🧵

✅ getAcquire = "subscribe". The reader says: "Give me the published value — and I'll correctly see everything written before it." Like opening the finished post, never a half-written draft. 🧵

✅ setRelease + getAcquire work as a pair: if the reader sees the flag, it also sees all the data written before the flag. 🧵

✅ setOpaque = the cheapest option: "I'm the only writer — just keep my own writes in order." No heavy cross-thread coordination. 🧵

✅ Bottom line: volatile does all of this at once, but expensively. VarHandle lets you pick only the guarantee you need — handy in hot, performance-critical code. 🧵
#Java #Concurrency #JMM

💡 JUnit 5's Extension API replaces JUnit 4's `@RunWith` Runner and `@Rule`.

✅ One model: implement lifecycle hooks like `BeforeEachCallback` instead of an exclusive Runner
✅ `ParameterResolver` injects test dependencies — no base-class inheritance needed
✅ Extensions compose: stack several with `@ExtendWith` and they just work together
#Java #JUnit5 #Testing

Java tip💡: Use StringBuilder instead of String for concatenating strings in loops.

❌ String is immutable, making it inefficient in loops, especially large ones.

✅ StringBuilder is mutable and optimized for repeated string appends.
#Java #CodeTips

Java tip💡: always close resources like BufferedReader, InputStream, OutputStream, using try-with-resources to avoid memory leaks.

#Java #BestPractices

Java Tip #4 💡: Threads may cache variables values. Mark the variable with the 'volatile' keyword to ensure that any change is  seen by other threads. This is the most basic synchronization mechanism of Java. #Java #JavaTips #Multithreading
Java Tip #6 💡: Keep your class responsibilities focused. Follow the Single Responsibility Principle. #Java #OOP

💡Java tip: Use the Stream API for declarative collection processing.

✅ Express what you want, not how to do it, and avoid  writing verbose loops
#Java #Collections
Java Tip #9💡: Avoid excessive number of method parameters. Group related values into objects. #Java #Refactoring
Java Tip💡:  Use meaningful method names that reflect their purpose. #Java #CleanCode
💡Java: With polymorphism, you can replace the use of instanceof in your code as a tool to fork logic. Let objects decide their behavior. #Java #OO

Java Tip💡: Starting from Java 14 you can use "record" to create short, immutable, data-carrying objects.

✅ They are shorter than ordinary POJOs
✅ They have built-in equals(), hashCode(), toString()
✅ They are immutable by default
#Java #Records
Java Tip #14💡: Enable compiler warnings and treat them as errors. They help prevent bugs early. #Java #BestPractices
Java Tip #15💡: Use logging frameworks like SLF4J instead of `System.out.println`. #Java #Logging

💡Java tip: Starting from Java 8 prefer java.time over Date and Calendar.

#ModernJava #DevTips
Java Tip #17💡: Avoid public fields. Use private fields with getters/setters for encapsulation. #Java #OOP

💡 Java tip: Catch mistakes at compile time by annotating overridden methods with @Override.
#Java #Annotations

code:
enum DataScope {
    CUSTOMERS_READ,
    CUSTOMERS_WRITE,
    CUSTOMERS_UPDATE,
    CUSTOMERS_DELETE,
    ORDERS_READ,
    ORDERS_WRITE,
    ORDERS_UPDATE,
    ORDERS_DELETE,
    PRODUCTS_READ,
    PRODUCTS_WRITE,
    PRODUCTS_UPDATE,
    PRODUCTS_DELETE,
    INVENTORY_READ,
    INVENTORY_WRITE,
    INVENTORY_UPDATE,
    INVENTORY_DELETE,
    PAYMENTS_READ,
    PAYMENTS_WRITE,
    PAYMENTS_UPDATE,
    PAYMENTS_DELETE,
    SHIPMENTS_READ,
    SHIPMENTS_WRITE,
    SHIPMENTS_UPDATE,
    SHIPMENTS_DELETE,
    INVOICES_READ,
    INVOICES_WRITE,
    INVOICES_UPDATE,
    INVOICES_DELETE,
    REPORTS_READ,
    REPORTS_WRITE,
    REPORTS_UPDATE,
    REPORTS_DELETE,
    USERS_READ,
    USERS_WRITE,
    USERS_UPDATE,
    USERS_DELETE,
    ROLES_READ,
    ROLES_WRITE,
    ROLES_UPDATE,
    ROLES_DELETE,
    AUDIT_READ,
    AUDIT_WRITE,
    AUDIT_UPDATE,
    AUDIT_DELETE,
    CONFIG_READ,
    CONFIG_WRITE,
    CONFIG_UPDATE,
    CONFIG_DELETE,
}

// 48 flags — EnumSet: bit vector (~48 bits)
// HashSet: 48 entries + buckets + boxing overhead

// Build the full set
EnumSet<DataScope> admin = EnumSet.allOf(DataScope.class);
Set<DataScope> adminHash = new HashSet<>(Arrays.asList(DataScope.values()));

// Bulk change — bitwise OR/AND on a few machine words
EnumSet<DataScope> ordersDomain = EnumSet.of(
    DataScope.ORDERS_READ, DataScope.ORDERS_WRITE,
    DataScope.ORDERS_UPDATE, DataScope.ORDERS_DELETE);
admin.removeAll(ordersDomain);

EnumSet<DataScope> readOnly = EnumSet.copyOf(admin);
readOnly.removeIf(p -> !p.name().endsWith("_READ"));

// contains() — O(1) bit test, not hash + bucket walk
boolean ok = admin.contains(DataScope.PAYMENTS_READ);

body:
Java Tip #20💡: Deep chains like user → address → city are a classic NPE trap. Nested if (x != null) blocks get verbose fast — and it's easy to miss a level.

✅ Optional: findByEmail(email).map(User::getAddress).map(Address::getCity).orElse("unknown")
✅ No Optional in the API? One guard: if (user == null || user.getAddress() == null)
✅ Either beats scattered null checks that hide what may actually be missing

#Java #NullSafety

💡Java Tip: Avoid magic numbers. In Java, a Magic Number refers to a hard-coded numeric value used in code without any explanation of what it represents.

Magic numbers reduce readability and make maintenance harder.

#Java #CleanCode

💡 Java tip: Use interface default methods to handle backward compatibility (starting from Java 8).
#Java #Interfaces
💡Java Patterns: The Builder Pattern allows for flexible, clear construction of complex objects. #Java #DesignPatterns
Java Tip 💡: Avoid catching generic `Exception` or `Throwable`. Be specific. #Java #ErrorHandling

💡 Java 8+ CompletableFuture: chain async steps without blocking the thread on every I/O call.

✅ Chain: supplyAsync → thenApply → thenCompose → thenAccept (fetch user, then orders)
✅ Handle failures with exceptionally — don't let async errors disappear silently
✅ Run independent calls in parallel, merge with thenCombine when both finish

#Java #Concurrency
Java Tip 💡: Validate user input. Never trust external input directly. #Java #Security
Java Tip 💡: Prefer immutable objects to ensure thread safety. #Java #Concurrency

💡 Java tip:  Clean up unused imports and variables for better readability.

#Java #CleanCode
Java Tip 💡: Use `try-catch` blocks sparingly—don't use them for flow control. #Java #BestPractices
Java Tip 💡: Avoid deeply nested if-else statements. Use guard clauses instead. #Java #CleanCode
Java Tip 💡: Split large methods into smaller helper methods for clarity. #Java #Refactoring

Java Tip 💡: When possible, avoid returning mutable internal collections from methods, so that:

1) You don't expose internal state, improving security
2) You can change the implementation without breaking other parts
#Java #JavaDev
Java tip💡: Catch exceptions at the right level, not too early or too late. #Java #ErrorHandling

Java tip 💡: You can use List.of() and Set.of() to create immutable collections.

These are a quick ways to create unmodifiable lists and sets without using Collections.unmodifiableList().

#Java #Collections
Java Tip 💡: Learn and follow Java naming conventions for classes, variables, and methods. #Java #CleanCode
Java tip💡: Avoid using `Thread.sleep()` for timing. Use proper synchronization or schedulers. #Java #Concurrency
Java Tip 💡: Understand the difference between `==` and `.equals()` for object comparison. #Java #Basics
Java Tip 💡: Always override `hashCode()` when you override `equals()`. #Java #OOP
Java Tip 💡: Use `Optional.ifPresentOrElse()` for elegant null handling (Java 9+). #Java #Optional
Java Tip 💡: Avoid exposing internal object state. Use DTOs when needed. #Java #Encapsulation

Java Tip 💡: Understand the difference between checked and unchecked exceptions.
#Java #ErrorHandling // Checked

Java Tip 💡: Use helper classes to implement reusable logic, but avoid all-knowing God classes.
#Java #CodeOrganization

#Java #CleanCode
Java Tip 💡: Refactor legacy code gradually. Use tests to ensure stability. #Java #LegacyCode
Java tip💡: Use 'volatile' to declare simple flags shared across threads. #Java #Multithreading
Java tip 💡: Don’t reinvent the wheel—use proven Java libraries and frameworks. #Java #Productivity
💡 Use `Pattern` and `Matcher` for complex regex operations. #Java #Regex
Java tip 💡: Format your code consistently. Use tools like Checkstyle or Spotless. #Java #CleanCode
Java Tip 💡: Avoid initializing objects you may never use. Lazy-load when appropriate. #Java #Performance
Java Tip 💡: Use `Deque` over `Stack`—it’s more modern and flexible. #Java #Collections

💡Java Tip : Prefer constructor injection over field injection.
#JavaTips
Java Tip 💡: Use `Comparator.comparing()` for sorting with lambdas. #Java #FunctionalProgramming
💡 Measure code coverage but don’t obsess over 100%. #Java #Testing
Java Tip 💡: Test edge cases—nulls, empty lists, and extreme values. #Java #Testing
Java Tip 💡:  Don’t overuse inheritance. Favor delegation when possible. Inheritance creates tight coupling, and this can lead to problems if the superclass changes. #Java #OOP
Java tip💡: Use `String.format()` or `MessageFormat` for readable formatting. #Java #StringTips

Java tip 💡: Don’t rely blindly on Garbage Collection; memory leaks are still possible.
#Java #SoftwareEngineering
Java Tip 💡: Use enums for state machines—it improves readability. #Java #StatePattern
💡 Cache expensive operations when possible. Use `ConcurrentMap` if needed. #Java #Performance
💡 Explore virtual threads (Java 19+) for lightweight concurrency. #Java #ProjectLoom
Java Tip 💡: Use `Files.walk()` to recursively process files in a directory. #Java #IO

body: Java Tip 💡: Make use of annotations like `@NotNull`, `@Nullable` for better tooling support. #Java #Annotations
💡 Learn design patterns to write reusable, maintainable code. #Java #DesignPatterns
Java Tip 💡: Refactor large switch statements into polymorphic classes. #Java #Refactoring
Java tip:💡: Use thread pools (`Executors`) instead of manually managing threads. #Java #Concurrency

Java Tip 💡: Master the SOLID principles to write cleaner, more maintainable object-oriented code.
Each letter stands for a key design principle:

S: Single Responsibility – A class should have only one reason to change.

O: Open/Closed – Classes should be open for extension, but closed for modification.

L: Liskov Substitution – Subtypes should be replaceable for their base types without breaking behavior.

I: Interface Segregation – Prefer many small, specific interfaces over one large general-purpose interface.

D: Dependency Inversion – Depend on abstractions, not concrete implementations.

Applying SOLID improves code flexibility, testability, and scalability.
#Java #CleanCode #OOP #SoftwareDesign
Java tip:💡: Avoid synchronized blocks when higher-level abstractions exist. #Java #Concurrency
Java Tip : To populate Map values, like lists, you can use computeIfAbsent(). #Java #Collections

Java tip 💡:

If you want to count the occurrences of an element in a list, you can use Collections.frequency(...).

#Java #Collections

Java tip 💡: Instead of using HashSet for enum types, consider using EnumSet, it's faster and more memory-efficient.

✅Example:

EnumSet<Month> months = EnumSet.of(Month.JANUARY, Month.APRIL);

#Java #Collections

Java tip💡: If you have a list of elements, you want to remove duplicates, and sort them in natural order or by a custom comparator, you can use TreeSet.

#Java #Collections

Java Tip 💡: You can use LinkedHashMap to easily implement an LRU (Least Recently Used) Cache.
#Java #Collections
Java tip : Starting from Java 19 you can explore a feature called "virtual threads". Virtual threads are lightweight threads managed by the JVM rather than the OS. #Java #Concurrency
Java tip 💡: Return Collections.emptyList(), List.of() or Set.of() instead of null collections to avoid null checks in calling code. #JavaBestPractice

Java tip 💡: Try to avoid sharing data across threads.

✅Use immutable objects
✅Or make threads communicate through messages, without directly modifying shared state.

#Java #Concurrency

Java Tip 💡: Override hashCode() when overriding equals() to ensure collections like HashMap work correctly.

HashMap is based on an hash table data structure, and uses hashCode() to determine the bucket index.

#Java #Collections

💡Java tip: Starting from Java 8,  for parallel processing of collections you can use parallelStream().

✅ Uses multiple threads
✅ Good for CPU-intensive processing
⚠️ Sharing mutable state is not safe
#Java #JavaDev

🚀 Open/Closed Principle (OCP): OCP is a SOLID principle that states classes should be open for extension but closed for modification.

✅Instead of touching existing implementation, you should use Java interfaces and inheritance to add new behavior.
#Java #JavaDev

💡Java tip: Use HashMap as Map implementation when you need greatest general-purpose performance.

#Java   #DataStructures

💡 SOLID Principles: Interface Segregation Principle (ISP) states that clients shouldn’t depend on methods they don’t use.

✅ So called "fat"interfaces (large, general-purpose interfaces) should be broken down into smaller, more specific ones. Classes shouldn't be forced to implement methods that are irrelevant to them.

#Java #JavaDev

💡SOLID principles: Dependency Inversion Principle (DIP) states that high-level modules should not depend on low-level modules; instead, both should depend on abstractions.

#SOLID #SoftwareDesign

Java tip: Starting from Java 11, if you need to repeat n times a specific string, you can use the String method repeat(n). Example: String result = "$".repeat(10); System.out.println(result); // $$$$$$$$$$ #Java #JavaTips
Java tip: Starting from java 9 you can use Stream.takeWhile() for easy slicing of lists by streams.  #Java #JavaTips
Java tip: You can use Collectors.joining(", ") to turn a stream into a comma-separated string (or another custom separator).  #Java #JavaTips
Java tip: From Java 9 you can use Objects.checkIndex() to replace manual if checks for validating index bounds. It is a small feature that helps making the code cleaner.  #Java #JavaTip

Java tip : Avoid static variables unless they store truly global values. They hinder testing by making it harder to isolate classes due to shared state. It can cause subtle bugs. For example: public class BankAccount { public static double balance; public BankAccount(double initialBalance) { balance = initialBalance; } public void deposit(double amount) { balance += amount; } public void printBalance() { System.out.println("Balance: " + balance); } } BankAccount acc1 = new BankAccount(1000); BankAccount acc2 = new BankAccount(500); acc1.deposit(200); acc2.printBalance(); // Balance: 700 (should be 500) The balance variable is shared globally, and changing it in one object affects the others. #Java #JavaTips
Java tip: Use List.copyOf(list) for a safe, unmodifiable copy, while avoiding accidental changes.  #Java #JavaTips
Java tip: Starting from Java 8 you can use List.removeIf(<predicate>) to clean up collections in one line. #JavaTips

💡Java tip: Use ThreadLocal.withInitial(...) to provide each thread its own safe default value.

✅ The value is created lazily
#Java #JavaDev

💡 Java tip: Starting from Java 8, you can remove nulls easily with list.removeIf(Objects::isNull).

#Java #JavaTips

💡 Java tip: Starting from Java 12, you can use String.indent(n)  to format multi-line strings with indentation.

💡 Java tip: Stream.toArray(Type[]::new) is a clean, type-safe way to get arrays from streams.

✅ Avoids Object[] and casting.

💡 Java tip: Starting from Java 8 you can use ChronoUnit.between(start, end) to calculate time intervals in a specific unit (days, hours, minutes) without manual math.

💡Java tip: Use Collectors.groupingBy(...) to group stream results by a classifier.

💡 Java tip: Use Stream.peek() for debugging in pipelines, not for business logic.

✅ Stream.peek() lets you perform logging or inspection on each element as it flows through a stream without modifying it.

💡 Java tip: List.reversed() returns an unmodifiable reversed view of the original list. #Java #JavaDev

💡 Java tip: Starting from Java 12 you can use String.transform(Function) to apply lambdas directly to strings.
#Java #JavaDev

💡 Java tip: Use Optional.filter(...) to add inline conditions to an Optional object.

✅ It lets you apply a condition directly and it returns the original Optional if the predicate passes, an empty one otherwise.
#Java #JavaDev

✅ If one is null, returns false
✅ If neither is null, calls a.equals(b)

💡 Sample repo to learn Spring Boot: github.com/spring-projects/spring-boot-samples

✅ 65+ runnable mini-apps
✅ Spring Boot 3.3, WebFlux, Security, Kafka, GraalVM
✅ Official Spring team code

The Spring Boot Samples repository is an official collection of over 65 runnable mini-applications maintained by the Spring team. Each sample demonstrates a specific feature:

✅ Basic REST APIs
✅ Spring WebFlux
✅ Spring Security
✅ Kafka messaging

#SpringBoot #Java #Kafka #Microservices #GraalVM #DevCommunity

💡Spring Boot tip: Use
@Profile
 to group and load beans/configurations in specific environments.

🚀 Spring Boot tip: With Spring Boot and Spring Security you can secure endpoints easily.

#SpringBoot #SpringSecurity #Java

💡 Spring Boot Tip: If you want DTOs to ignore extra JSON fields from clients without breaking your API use @JsonIgnoreProperties(ignoreUnknown = true).

#SpringBoot #CodingTips #SoftwareEngineering

💡 Spring Boot tip: Spring Boot prints a banner in the console when your app starts. You can customize it for branding or just fun.

✅ Add a file banner.txt  in src/main/resources and put whatever content you want.

✅ You can also use placeholders like ${spring-boot.version} or ${java.version} to inject values inside it.

✅ If you want, you can disable the banner with the following configuration property:

spring.main.banner-mode=off

#Java #SpringBoot

💡 Spring Boot tip: You can run jobs at fixed intervals without setting up Quartz or external schedulers.
#Java #SpringBoot

🚀Spring Boot DevTools is a tool to ease developer's work a bit. It watches code and configuration for changes, then automatically reloads or restarts your app.
#SpringBoot #SoftwareDevelopment

🚀 Spring Boot tip: In development, you can package and run an application from the source code with just one command:

✅ When you’re in development, you can skip packaging and just run directly:

./mvnw spring-boot:run

✅ If you want, you can combine packaging and run in one command:

./mvnw clean package && java -jar target/myapp-0.0.1-SNAPSHOT.jar

#Java #SpringBoot

🚀Spring Boot: Use TaskScheduler to implement lightweight dynamic tasks (consider Quartz for more complex requirements).
#SpringBoot #SoftwareDevelopment

💡 Spring Boot tip: Use CommandLineRunner for executing startup logic.

CommandLineRunner is a Spring Boot interface that can be implemented to run code after the application context is fully initialized.

#SpringBoot #Java

💡 Spring Boot tip: Use spring-boot-starter-logging (Logback) for logging, it is already included as default.

✅ spring-boot-starter-logging is already included in most starters (spring-boot-starter-web, spring-boot-starter-data-jpa, etc.)

✅ You can customize log levels by editing application.yml:

logging:
  level:
    root: INFO
    com.myapp: DEBUG

✅ To customize format and rotation, create a logback-spring.xml in src/main/resources.

#SpringBoot #Java

💡Spring Boot tip: Use H2 if you need an in-memory   database for dev/testing.

    #SpringBoot #Java

🚀Spring Boot tip: Boost performance in Spring Boot with @EnableCaching and
@Cacheable
.

🟢 Avoids repeated DB/API calls
🟢 Reduces latency
🟢 By default, Spring uses an in-memory map, but you  can plug in an external provider for production.
#SpringBoot #Performance

💡 Spring Boot tip: use
@Transactional
 at the service layer, not on repositories.

✅  You coordinate business logic in the service layer, and a service method:

🟢 Calls one or more repositories
🟢 Implements business rules
🟢 Needs everything to succeed or fail together
#SpringBoot

💡 Spring Boot tip: Use @SpringBootTest(webEnvironment = RANDOM_PORT) for integration tests when testing controllers.

✅ Starts a real embedded server
✅ The server runs on a random port.
✅ You can test controllers via TestRestTemplate or WebTestClient.
#SpringBoot

🚀 Spring Boot: springdoc-openapi automatically generates OpenAPI 3.0 (Swagger) documentation from your Spring Boot REST controllers.

You will get:

✅ An interactive Swagger UI
✅ Auto-scanned from your code
✅ Export API docs in JSON/YAML
#SpringBoot

🚀 Spring Boot: You can enable a smooth shutdown with "server.shutdown=graceful" in your configuration.

✅  It helps you avoid these situations:

🔴 Active HTTP requests failing abruptly
🔴 Database rolling back unexpectedly
🔴 Threads interrupted before finishing
#SpringBoot

💡 Pin the Spring Boot version in the spring-boot-dependencies BOM to ensure consistent dependency management.

✅  The spring-boot-dependencies BOM allows you to omit versions of libraries that Spring Boot integrates with in your pom.xml.
#SpringBoot

🚀 Spring Boot: Use ResponseEntity<T> to get fine-grained control over HTTP responses.

You can explicitly specify Status, Headers and Body
#SpringBoot

🚀  Spring Boot: Use spring-boot-starter-mail for sending emails.

✅ It wraps all the necessary configuration and dependencies, making the process of sending emails simple and clear.
#SpringBoot

💡 Spring Boot: To better handle circular dependencies, use constructor injection instead of field injection.

✅ Forces all required dependencies to be provided at creation time, allowing early detection.
#SpringBoot #JavaDev

🚀 Spring Boot: Use @SpringBootApplication(exclude = …) to disable specific auto-configuration classes.

✅ One possible reason could be that you have a custom configuration that conflicts with the default.
#SpringBoot #JavaDev

🚀 Spring Boot: If you need to inject grouped configuration properties into classes, you can use @ConfigurationProperties instead of @Value
#SpringBoot #JavaDev

🚀 Spring Boot: In development phase, use spring.main.lazy-initialization=true to speed up the startup of the application.
#SpringBoot #JavaDev

🚀 Spring Boot: You can validate your input DTO objects with @Valid + @NotBlank, @Email, etc.
#Java #SoftwareDevelopment

🚀 Spring Boot: Spring Data JPA has a built-in support for pagination with Pageable.

✅You should use pagination in your repositories instead of fetching all.
#SpringBoot #JavaDev

💡Spring Boot tip: If you need to implement integrations tests for Spring MVC controllers, and mock everything else, use @WebMvcTest to load only MVC-related beans.

#SpringBoot #IntegrationTesting

🚀 Spring Boot: You can use @Retryable to cope with unstable external services.
#SpringBoot #SoftwareDevelopment

🚀 Spring Boot: With @ConditionalOnMissingBean, you can define a default bean that can be possibly overridden by a user using a standard definition.
#SpringBoot #SoftwareDevelopment

🚀Spring Boot: You have the choice of using WebClient over RestTemplate in new apps.

✅ It’s reactive and non-blocking
✅ Based on event-loop instead of thread-per-request
✅ Especially fitted for microservices
#SpringBoot #JavaDev

🚀 SpringBoot: Add global OpenAPI metadata with @OpenAPIDefinition.

✅ It should be put on your main Spring Boot application class, or a dedicated configuration class
#SpringBoot #JavaDev

💡 SpringBoot: Define @ComponentScan boundaries carefully to avoid to avoid scanning entire packages accidentally.
#SpringBoot #JavaDev

🚀 Spring Boot: You can cache expensive API calls using
@Cacheable
 and Redis.

✅First request with a specific key: real API call
✅Subsequent requests: Redis (milliseconds)
#SpringBoot #JavaDev

🚀 Spring Boot: If your code uses RestTemplate, you can implement tests by mocking external calls with MockRestServiceServer.

✅ MockRestServiceServer works by intercepting HTTP requests
#SpringBoot #JavaDev

🚀 Spring Boot: To make Jackson create your objects from JSON in an explicit, and predictable way, you can use @JsonCreator.

🟢 This way, each parameter is explicitly bound

#SpringBoot #JavaDev

🚀 Spring Boot:  To execute SQL scripts before or after a test method, you can use the @Sql annotation.
#SpringBoot #JavaDev

🚀 Spring Boot: If your application uses RestTemplate for REST calls, you can centralize timeouts with RestTemplateBuilder .
#SpringBoot #JavaDev

🚀Spring Boot: To exclude any fields whose value is null in Jackson serialization you can use @JsonInclude(Include.NON_NULL) .
#SpringBoot #JavaDev

🚀 Spring Boot: Better avoid wildcard exclusions for transitive dependencies in POMs, because they can hide problems.

❌ Wildcard exclusions just remove the symptom and mask the underlying version mismatch

#SpringBoot #JavaDev

🚀 Spring Boot: Keep controllers thin, put logic in services.

✅ Controllers main job is just to handle HTTP concerns

#SpringBoot #SoftwareEngineering

🚀 Spring Boot: Avoid FetchType.EAGER unless you really need it.

❌ Marking a relationship as EAGER tells the ORM to load it whenever the entity is loaded, regardless of whether you actually invoke the relationship's method.
#Springboot #SoftwareDevelopment

🚀 Spring Boot: You can implement optimistic locking using the @Version annotation.

✅ Ideal when:

Reads are frequent
Writes are relatively rare
Conflicts are uncommon

❌ Not Ideal when:

Conflicts are frequent
Strict serialization is needed
#SpringBoot #JavaDev

🚀 Spring Boot: Prefer static utility classes over Spring beans only if they don't hold state, they have no dependencies and they don't need mocking or AOP.
#SpringBoot #SoftwareDevelopment

🚀 Spring Boot:  Better not expose JPA entities directly, use DTOs at boundaries instead.

✅This allows to:

🟢 Make API independent on the persistence layer
🟢 Prevent lazy loading problems
🟢 Improve security

#SpringBoot #SoftwareEngineer

🚀 You can use async logging in Spring Boot by configuring logback (logback-spring.xml).

✅ Log messages are sent into a queue,  and are elaborated by a separate background thread.

✅ This reduces I/O bottlenecks.
#SpringBoot #JavaDev

🚀 Spring Boot: @AutoConfigureMockMvc allows you to test controllers without starting the server. It tells Spring Boot to automatically configure a MockMvc instance in the test context.
#SpringBoot #Java

Spring Boot: Use spring.mvc.publish-request-params=true only in dev. It's dangerous in production.

🔴In production, logging request details can expose sensitive information.

#SpringBoot #JavaDev

🚀 Spring Boot: Better keep the default spring.main.allow-bean-definition-overriding=false configuration.

✅This way Spring fails to start if two beans share the same name.

✅Useful because bean overriding can hide configuration bugs
#SpringBoot #JavaDev

🚀 Spring Boot: Better use global filters/interceptors only for true cross-cutting concerns, and avoid business logic. They are executed for every incoming HTTP request.
#SpringBoot #JavaDev

Spring Boot: Avoid importing the full spring-boot-starter-web if you only need Spring MVC, because the full starter brings in extra components like an embedded server that you may not need.
#SpringBoot #JavaDev

🚀 Spring Boot: You can use the "spring.jpa.properties.hibernate.jdbc.batch_size" property to make Hibernate handle batch inserts.

🟢Fewer network calls
🟢Less JDBC overhead
🟢Faster bulk operations
#SpringBoot #JavaDev

🚀 Spring Boot: You can enable /actuator/beans to debug missing beans.

By hitting the actuator endpoint, you will see information like:

✅ Bean name
✅ Bean type
✅ Bean scope
✅ Bean dependencies

#SpringBoot #JavaDev
code:
# application.yaml
management:
  endpoints:
    web:
      exposure:
        include: health, info, metrics
# -> GET /actuator/health

#SpringBoot #JavaDev

🚀 Spring Boot testing: Using spring.main.log-startup-info=false, you can skip the verbose startup summary.

✅ It makes your app fail fast at startup if the configuration is invalid.
